title: Comprehensive Protocol for Handling Human Small Airway Epithelial Cells (HSAECs) to Establish Air–Liquid Interface (ALI) Cultures With TEER-Based Barrier Integrity Assessment
author: Jakubczyk, Dominika

steps.title: A
steps.instruction: A. Initiating HSAEC culture from a preserved sample
1. Prepare materials and reagents

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Check the vial label for the number of cells in the backup sample.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Choose an appropriate culture flask based on a seeding density (5,000–10,000 cells/cm2).

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Calculate the required volume of complete growth medium, using 3–5 mL per 25 cm2 of flask surface area.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. Transfer the calculated volume of complete medium into a sterile tube and warm it to 37 °C in a water bath.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Always work under sterile conditions. Vials, tubes, and culture flasks must never be opened outside the safety cabinet. Prior to placing them inside, lightly mist their surfaces with 70% ethanol to minimise the risk of contamination. The medium warming time depends on the volume, e.g., 50 mL may take 20–30 min.
2. Thaw the frozen cells by gently agitating the cryovial in a 37 °C water bath for 1–2 min until the content is fully thawed.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Be aware that extending the cell thawing time can negatively impact cell viability.

steps.title: 3. Transfer and mix
steps.instruction: 3. Transfer and mix: Using a sterile pipette, transfer the thawed cell suspension into the prewarmed medium. Mix gently to ensure homogeneity.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Freshly thawed primary cells should not be centrifuged, as this may affect their viability and alter their ability to adhere.
4. Seed the cells

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Transfer the entire volume of cell suspension into the culture flask.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Incubate at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 and 20% O2 for 24 h.

steps.title: 5. Assess cell attachment
steps.instruction: 5. Assess cell attachment: After 24 h, inspect the flask under a microscope to evaluate cell adherence. If most cells are attached, proceed to medium replacement. If not, extend the incubation for another 12–14 h.
6. Replace the medium

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Prewarm fresh medium to 37 °C.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Carefully aspirate the medium using a serological pipette, avoiding disturbance of attached cells, and replace with fresh medium.
7. Continue the culture with confluency monitoring

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Return the flask to the incubator and maintain the standard culture conditions as previously described.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. After 24–48 h, assess cell confluency under a microscope. If confluency is <70%, continue replacing the medium every 48 h. If confluency reaches 70%–80%, proceed with cell passaging.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: HSAECs belong to contact-inhibited cells. Do not allow confluency to exceed 80%, as this may suppress proliferation and induce morphological changes that could compromise experimental outcomes. Figure 1 presents the confluence percentage of HSAEC cells in the culture flask.

steps.title: (A) At seeding
steps.instruction: (A) At seeding. (B) At 70%–80% confluency. (C) At >90% confluency. Scale bars, 100 μm.

steps.title: B
steps.instruction: B. HSAEC passage
1. Prepare materials and reagents. Determine the appropriate volume of culture medium needed based on the flask size, and warm it to 37 °C before use.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Since temperature enhances enzymatic activity and facilitates cell detachment, warm both the trypsin-EDTA solution and the trypsin neutralizing solution in a 37 °C water bath. Allow the D-PBS to reach room temperature.
2. Remove culture medium and rinse the flask bottom

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Carefully aspirate the culture medium, taking care not to disturb the attached cells.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Rinse the cell layer with 3–5 mL of D-PBS to remove residual medium, then aspirate completely.
3. Apply trypsin-EDTA and monitor detachment

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Add 2 mL of prewarmed trypsin-EDTA per 25 cm2 of surface area.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Gently rock the flask to ensure an even distribution across the cell layer.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Place the flask in a 37 °C incubator for 1–3 min.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. Then, observe the detached cells under a microscope; detached cells become round and float in the solution.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: If some cells remain attached, vigorously tap the bottom of the flask from multiple sides to facilitate detachment. Do not use any cell scraper as it damages the cells and reduces the number of viable cells. If needed, return the flask to the incubator for an additional 1–2 min. Do not exceed a total incubation time of 5 min, as prolonged exposure to trypsin can damage cells. Overexposure to trypsin may cause white streaks to appear in the solution; if this occurs, promptly neutralise the solution.
4. Neutralise trypsin and collect detached cells

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Once most cells are detached, add trypsin neutralising solution in an equal volume to the trypsin.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Gently pipette to ensure thorough mixing and complete neutralisation.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Transfer the cell suspension to a sterile centrifuge tube.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. To recover remaining cells, rinse the flask bottom 2–3 times with 5 mL of D-PBS and combine all the fractions with the original suspension.
5. Centrifuge and resuspend the obtained pellet in fresh medium

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Centrifuge the cell suspension at 150× g for 5 min to pellet the cells.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Carefully aspirate the supernatant and then resuspend the cell pellet in 1–3 mL of fresh, prewarmed complete growth medium, adjusting the volume based on the pellet size.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: It is important to note that when dealing with a small pellet, suspending it in a large volume of medium can excessively dilute the cells, potentially hindering accurate quantification by a cell counter. If the volume needs to be reduced afterwards, additional centrifugation steps may be required, which could adversely affect cell vitality and viability. To prevent this, it is advisable to begin with a small volume of medium (e.g., 1 mL) to resuspend the pellet, increasing the volume gradually only if needed. Figure 2 illustrates representative cell pellet sizes after centrifugation and the recommended medium volumes for resuspension.

steps.title: (A) Smaller pellet size; recommended 1...
steps.instruction: (A) Smaller pellet size; recommended 1 mL of medium for resuspension. (B) More compact pellet; recommended 2–3 mL of medium for resuspension.
6. Count the cells

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Transfer equal volumes of cell suspension and Trypan Blue (10 μL + 10 μL, respectively) to an Eppendorf tube.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Collect 10 μL of the mixture and pipette it onto the slide for measurement, ensuring that no air bubbles form in the measuring window.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Place the slide in the cell counter and read the calculated number of viable cells per 1 mL of suspension.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Depending on the planned experiments and the number of cells required, seed the cells into a larger flask (150–300 cm2) and continue culturing until the desired cell number is achieved. Alternatively, if the number of cells is already sufficient, begin the ALI culture.

steps.title: C
steps.instruction: C. Establishing ALI culture
1. Prepare materials and reagents

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Prior to initiating the culture under ALI conditions, collect and count the cells following the procedure outlined above.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Determine the required number of inserts for the planned experiments, ensuring sufficient cell quantity, bearing in mind that each insert requires 0.1 × 106 cells (final density insert directly is approximately 3.0 × 105 cells/cm2).

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Conduct all steps under sterile conditions within a biosafety cabinet. To disinfect the tweezers, transfer 5–10 mL of 70% ethanol into a 15 mL centrifuge tube and submerge them in it for 5–10 min.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. Remove the tweezers and allow them to air dry. Avoid contact with any other equipment or surfaces to maintain sterility.

steps.title: e
steps.instruction: e. Prepare the required number of inserts and culture plates in advance. Open the seal and, using sterile tweezers, grasp one of the three designated handles to carefully transfer the insert into the wells of the culture plate.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Maintain strict aseptic technique throughout the process. Avoid placing inserts in edge wells; instead, fill those wells with 1 mL of sterile water to maintain high humidity and minimise the risk of cell desiccation. To enhance workflow efficiency, particularly when handling multiple plates with inserts, use a disinfected, lidded plastic container (plastic box) as a container for all plates.

steps.title: f
steps.instruction: f. After completing the seeding procedure, place all plates inside the container, along with an open centrifuge tube containing 2–3 mL of sterile water, to help maintain humidity. Keep the container in the incubator with the lid slightly ajar.
2. Seed the cells in liquid–liquid culture

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Pipette 0.1 × 106 cells suspended in 400 μL of complete medium into the interior of each insert.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Add 1 mL of complete medium directly into the well beneath the insert to maintain liquid–liquid culture conditions for 7 days (at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 and 20% O2).

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Leave one insert unseeded and handle it identically to the others containing cells. This insert will serve as a blank control for TEER measurements.

steps.title: d. Replace the medium in all inserts every 48 h
steps.instruction: d. Replace the medium in all inserts every 48 h: 400 μL in the insert and 1 mL in the well.
3. Establish ALI culture

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. After 7 days of liquid–liquid culture, carefully remove the culture medium from the wells and inserts (without damaging the cell layer in the insert).

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Add 270 μL of culture medium to the well and incubate at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 and 20% O2.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Change the medium every 48 h.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. Place the plastic container holding all plates and the tube filled with sterile water in the incubator, ensuring the lid remains slightly open to maintain humidity.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: The height of plates and inserts may vary slightly depending on the batch and/or manufacturer. Therefore, the volume of medium added beneath the insert under ALI conditions may need to be adjusted. The bottom of the insert should be slightly submerged in the culture medium. If, 24 h after adding 270 μL beneath the insert, medium accumulates inside the insert, carefully remove the excess. When replacing the medium, reduce the volume added beneath the insert by the amount previously collected from its interior. Carefully check for air bubbles under the insert.
4. Continue the ALI culture with cell integrity monitoring

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Keep the plates in the incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2, 20% O2, humidified conditions).

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Replace the medium beneath the inserts every 48 h (around 270 μL).

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. To monitor barrier integrity, measure TEER at least once a week, or more frequently if necessary. Figure 3 illustrates the morphology of HSAECs grown on inserts across different time points.

steps.title: (A) Empty membrane
steps.instruction: (A) Empty membrane. (B) Cells after seeding on the insert. (C) First day of air–liquid interface (ALI) culture. (D) Twenty-first day of ALI culture. Scale bars, 100 µm. Magnification: 4× (A) and 10× (B–D).

steps.title: D
steps.instruction: D. TEER measurement
1. Prepare materials and reagents

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Prewarm the appropriate volume of complete medium in a water bath set to 37 °C.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Temperature plays a critical role in the accuracy of the readings: elevated temperatures increase medium conductance, leading to reduced resistance values, whereas a drop in temperature can transiently raise TEER. Consequently, any temperature fluctuation between measurements may introduce considerable variability in the recorded results. Therefore, standardisation of the procedure is crucial. To ensure consistency across all experiments and replicates, the culture medium temperature in both wells and inserts must be comparable between each TEER measurement.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Disinfect the electrode by transferring 5–7 mL of 70% ethanol to a 15 mL centrifuge tube and submerging it for 2–3 min.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Remove the electrode from the ethanol and let it air dry completely.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. Repeat the process using 5–7 mL of prewarmed complete medium to condition the electrode and allow it to air-dry as previously.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: During this process, avoid touching the electrode to maintain sterility. Note that the electrode height is adjustable. For accurate TEER measurements, both electrode arms must be fully immersed in the culture medium. The longer arm should gently reach the bottom of the well, while the shorter arm should not contact the insert membrane, as this could disrupt the cell layer. It is essential to determine the optimal electrode height experimentally before the first reading (e.g., in the blank well) and maintain that setting throughout all subsequent measurements. Figure 4 illustrates proper and improper electrode placement in the insert/well.

steps.title: e
steps.instruction: e. Set up the Volt-Ohm Meter Millicell TEER measurement unit by powering on the device and inserting the test electrode to begin calibration. The display should read 1,000 Ω; if it does not, adjust the settings manually to match the correct value.

steps.title: f
steps.instruction: f. After calibration, plug in the measurement electrode and wait a few minutes for stabilisation; the unit is now ready to begin recording TEER values.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: The operation of TEER measurement devices may vary depending on the manufacturer. If you are using equipment different from the one specified in this protocol, please follow the manufacturer's instructions for proper use and calibration.

steps.title: (A) Correct position
steps.instruction: (A) Correct position. (B–D) Examples of incorrect electrode position: (B) electrode held at an angle; (C) short electrode arm contacting the membrane/cell layer; (D) electrode contacting the insert walls.
2. Replace the culture medium

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Remove the culture medium from the well.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Add 400 μL to the insert and 1 mL to the well of the prewarmed culture medium and keep the plates in the incubator for 15 min to stabilise.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Place the plate outside the safety cabinet for an additional 15 min to allow the temperature to equalise.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: These steps ensure that a similar measurement temperature is maintained for all measurements throughout the ALI culture.
3. Measure the resistance

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. Begin by measuring the baseline resistance using the blank insert (without cells). Carefully immerse the electrode, ensuring the shorter arm is placed inside the insert and the longer arm in the well below.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Take care not to touch the edges of the insert and always position the electrode vertically at a 90° angle relative to the well surface. Allow a moment for the reading to stabilise; the resistance values may fluctuate initially but should settle within approximately 1 min.

steps.title: b
steps.instruction: b. Once the resistance value is stable, write down the value obtained.

steps.title: c
steps.instruction: c. Proceed to measure the resistance in the remaining inserts using the same technique.

steps.title: d
steps.instruction: d. After completing the first round of measurements, repeat the process to have at least two independent readings per well. Use the average values to calculate the final resistance.

steps.title: e
steps.instruction: e. After finishing the measurement, clean the electrode by immersing it in 70% ethanol for 2–3 min and then allow it to air-dry.
4. Calculate the TEER value

steps.title: a
steps.instruction: a. The TEER (Ωcm2) formula is based on the NET resistance, which is the resistance (Ω) measured in cell-covered insert minus the resistance of a blank well and multiplied by the surface area of the insert (in cm2).

steps.title: Example:
steps.instruction: Example:

steps.title: Value measured for blank insert
steps.instruction: Value measured for blank insert: 280 Ω

steps.title: Value measured for cells covered insert
steps.instruction: Value measured for cells covered insert: 350 Ω

steps.title: The information on the insert’s surface...
steps.instruction: The information on the insert’s surface area (cm2) is available on the insert box’s specification label or on the manufacturer's webpage. For inserts suitable for a 24-well plate, the surface area is 0.33 cm2.

steps.title: The TEER value for the tested insert is 23
steps.instruction: The TEER value for the tested insert is 23.1 Ωcm2.

steps.title: Since TEER values are closely linked...
steps.instruction: Since TEER values are closely linked to tight junction expression, the presence of ZO-1, claudin-1, and claudin-4 was assessed on days 12 and 21 using western blot. Representative results are shown in Figure 6.

steps.title: Western blotting was performed using ZO-1,...
steps.instruction: Western blotting was performed using ZO-1, claudin-1, claudin-4, and β-actin antibodies at a 1:1,000 dilution, in accordance with established protocols [10].
